video_title: What's the Left's Vision for Foreign Policy After Trump? | The Ezra Klein Show
gist: Matt Duss, foreign policy adviser to Bernie Sanders and AOC, argues that the Democratic Party faces a generational reckoning on Gaza analogous to the Iraq War rupture, and lays out a left foreign policy vision centered on ending unconditional military aid to Israel, reasserting Congressional war powers, rejecting American military primacy, cooperating rather than competing with China, and building a 'foreign policy for the working class' rooted in anti-corruption and a positive-sum global trade order. The conversation diagnoses elite impunity and the arms-lobby-driven security state as structural root causes, not merely Biden-era missteps.

themes.theme: Gaza as Democratic Party Litmus Test and 2028 Primary Fault Line

insights.claim: Senator Brian Schatz of Hawaii — widely seen as a potential next Senate Democratic leader — publicly called for a complete turnover of foreign policy staffers in the next Democratic administration, signaling that Gaza has become a personnel accountability issue at the highest levels of party leadership.
insights.speaker: Ezra Klein (quoting Schatz)
insights.so_what: If the next Democratic primary mirrors the 2008 Iraq War dynamic, incumbents and aspirants with pro-Israel voting records face serious primary vulnerability; donors and operatives tied to the existing foreign policy establishment face institutional exclusion.
insights.quote: I do think it's fair to want a whole new crop, a whole new crop of foreign policy staffers in the next Democratic administration. It's not like the same 120 people are the only people who know anything.

insights.claim: Senator Chris Van Holland wrote in the New York Times that Democratic primary voters will not support any 2028 presidential candidate who voted to send Netanyahu bombs during the Gaza blockade, or who plans to rehire senior Biden officials who 'whitewashed the truth' — using the word 'complicity' explicitly.
insights.speaker: Ezra Klein (quoting Van Holland)
insights.so_what: Creates a hard credential filter for the 2028 Democratic primary field that disqualifies much of the current foreign policy donor-advisory class; watch for primary positioning by potential candidates around Gaza votes.
insights.quote: Primary voters won't trust any Democratic presidential candidate who does not have a record of moral and strategic clarity on these issues, especially if, as a legislator, he or she voted to send Mr. Netanyahu bombs.

insights.claim: Gaza has already become a decisive issue in multiple Democratic primaries in 2026: Abdul El-Sayed leads in the Michigan Senate Democratic primary, Adam Hami (a doctor who treated Gaza wounded) won the New Jersey House primary, and Brad Lander leads Dan Goldman in the New York House primary — all running explicitly against U.S. support for Israel.
insights.speaker: Ezra Klein
insights.so_what: The electoral proof-of-concept that Gaza can win Democratic primaries is being established in 2026 races — the issue is no longer theoretical for the 2028 cycle; incumbents who supported Israel aid are structurally exposed.

insights.claim: Matt Duss argues that Kamala Harris's failure to convince Democratic voters on Gaza was not primarily a policy failure but a credibility failure — voters perceived her language ('too many civilians have died') as insincere — whereas Zohran Mamdani's willingness to hold his Gaza position under attack added to his overall credibility even with voters who didn't focus on the issue.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: For 2028 candidates, the lesson is that authenticity on Gaza matters more than the specific policy position; calculated triangulation is likely to be more damaging than a clear stance either way.
insights.quote: Even for people who perhaps didn't care about the issue all that much, they could tell that this was not genuine.

insights.claim: Duss draws an explicit parallel between Gaza and the Iraq War as a party-defining rupture but insists Gaza must produce actual accountability — naming officials who carried out Biden's Gaza policy as people who 'should not work in government again' — unlike Iraq, where no senior officials faced consequences.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: Signals an organized effort to build a blacklist of Biden-era national security officials, affecting the hiring market for Democratic foreign policy talent and the advisory ecosystem around 2028 campaigns.
insights.quote: I hope it doesn't become like the Iraq war because I don't think anybody really paid a price for the Iraq war, at least the officials who carried it out. I want to see some consequences for the people who carried out the Gaza policy.

themes.theme: Biden Administration Gaza Policy: Disinformation and Structural Capture

insights.claim: Duss argues the Biden administration ran a deliberate campaign of disinformation on Gaza — repeatedly claiming it could not assess whether Israel was violating international humanitarian law — while simultaneously assessing within one month that Russia was committing war crimes in Ukraine (Secretary Blinken's February 2022 statement).
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: The documented asymmetry between Ukraine and Gaza assessments is the sharpest accountability argument available to the left; it is likely to be used extensively in 2028 primary debates.
insights.quote: The idea that they could not make a similar assessment of a military into whose operations the United States has vastly more visibility I think is just just it's just not credible.

insights.claim: Duss traces Biden's unconditional Israel support to a personal, long-held doctrine that there should be 'no daylight' between the U.S. and Israel publicly, and that once Biden set those guardrails, staff stopped arguing and shaped policy within them — not a bureaucratic failure but a principal-level decision.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: Frames Gaza not as a systemic failure of the foreign policy bureaucracy but as a personal leadership failure by Biden, which has implications for how reformers target accountability — at the principal level, not just staff.
insights.quote: Once the boss has kind of laid down the parameters of where he or she is willing to go and not go, I think staffers start to tend to shape... you stop arguing and you say, 'Okay, these are the guardrails.'

insights.claim: Biden called conditioning military aid to Israel a 'preposterous idea' during the 2020 Democratic primary when Sanders raised it; by 2026 both AOC and Rahm Emanuel had publicly endorsed ending aid to Israel, illustrating how rapidly the Overton window has shifted.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: The speed of Overton window movement on Israel aid — from 'preposterous' in 2020 to bipartisan endorsement by 2026 — suggests this is a durable policy shift, not a temporary protest position; it has real implications for the U.S.-Israel defense industrial relationship.

insights.claim: Duss draws a direct causal line from the Abraham Accords — by sidelining the Palestinian issue and demonstrating that Arab normalization could proceed without a Palestinian state — to Hamas's October 7 attack, arguing the Accords were a key factor in Hamas's calculation that it needed to force the issue back onto the global agenda.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: The Abraham Accords — still viewed as a diplomatic success in most of Washington — are being reframed by the left as a proximate cause of the October 7 attack; this reframing will shape how a left-led administration treats normalization frameworks going forward.
insights.quote: It was one of the factors that led to Hamas's thinking about why they needed to take action, horrific action, no doubt, to kind of put the Palestinian issue back on the regional and global agenda.

insights.claim: Sanders and AOC chose not to push Biden out despite their deep disagreement on Gaza partly because they had access on domestic/economic policy and partly because, as Duss suspects, they recognized that the progressive left pushing out the president would hand the centrist establishment a 'disunifying' narrative to use against them.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: Illustrates the structural weakness of the left within the Democratic coalition — they are willing to sacrifice a core policy objection (Gaza) to avoid being blamed for party division; this constraint shapes how aggressively left candidates will press foreign policy in 2028.
insights.quote: I think that kind of played into their hesitance as well.

themes.theme: Left Foreign Policy Blueprint: Aid, Arms Laws, and Palestinian Governance

insights.claim: Duss's core Israel policy prescription is: (1) end all U.S. military aid to Israel (which he notes AOC and then Rahm Emanuel both endorsed); (2) enforce existing U.S. law — the Leahy Law and Arms Export Control Act — which already prohibit arms sales to military units with proven human rights abuse records, laws that multiple administrations have simply ignored.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: Framing aid cuts as law enforcement rather than new policy is a strategically significant rhetorical move — it shifts the burden of proof and avoids the charge of anti-Israel bias; watch for this framing in congressional legislation.
insights.quote: We have laws on the books that condition aid to every country according to a set of principles... We have simply not upheld those laws.

insights.claim: Duss argues that Hamas disarmament can only occur under legitimate Palestinian self-governance, not occupation, and that any durable Gaza settlement requires a tacit agreement with Hamas since it remains a relevant force that military operations have failed to destroy.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: Positions the left as willing to engage with Hamas as a political actor — a major departure from current U.S. policy that treats Hamas purely as a terrorist group; this will be a central fault line in the 2028 primary.
insights.quote: The disarmament of Hamas will never happen under a situation of occupation. It will only conceivably happen under a situation of legitimate Palestinian self-governance.

insights.claim: Duss advocates for sanctions against Israeli officials who support settlement expansion, arguing that U.S. policy has imposed disincentives exclusively on Palestinians while imposing zero meaningful costs on Israel, which has rationally concluded it can press forward with de facto annexation indefinitely.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: Expansion of targeted sanctions beyond the Biden administration's limited violent-settler sanctions would directly affect Israeli government officials and potentially Israeli defense companies; a signal to watch in any future Democratic administration.
insights.quote: There are no consequences that are ever imposed on the more powerful side, the Israeli side. And I think this dynamic has really given Israel a very reasonable belief that they can just press forward with de facto annexation.

insights.claim: Duss cites the Saudi/UAE war in Yemen as an exact precedent for why the 'stay engaged to have influence' argument for continuing to arm Israel fails: Obama administration officials themselves later acknowledged in a public letter (supporting the Sanders-Murphy-Lee war powers resolution on Yemen) that staying supportive of Saudi Arabia gave no meaningful influence and only affirmed a terrible war — and some of those same officials then made the identical argument for arming Israel in Gaza.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: The Yemen precedent undercuts the 'influence through engagement' defense at a factual level; expect this argument to be deployed in congressional hearings and primary debates.
insights.quote: Some of the people who signed that letter went on to serve in the Biden administration and are now out here offering the exact same argument for why it was better to continue supporting Netanyahu and Israel and Gaza. And I don't buy it.

themes.theme: Anti-War Electoral Politics and the 2024-2028 Opportunity

insights.claim: Duss argues that in every U.S. presidential election since the end of the Cold War, with the single exception of 2004, the more anti-war candidate has won — including Biden in 2020, who ran on ending the 'forever wars.' Democrats abandoned the anti-war lane in 2024, leaving it open for Trump to exploit.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: This electoral pattern, if durable, implies that the 2028 Democratic nominee should anchor their foreign policy on anti-interventionism; it is a structural argument for nominating someone from the Sanders-AOC-Murphy wing on foreign policy.
insights.quote: Democrats just abandoned the anti-war lane and left it wide open for Trump.

insights.claim: Congressman Jason Crow (D-CO, former Army officer) gave a speech at the Center for American Progress arguing that the core divide in U.S. politics is between those who see Trump as the cause of the crisis versus those who see him as a symptom of 30 years of elite failure — including 20+ years of failed military interventions costing $3 trillion, 3 million combat tours, and 7,000 American dead — and that the working class bore the unequal burden of this.
insights.speaker: Jason Crow (via clip)
insights.so_what: Crow's 'symptom vs. cause' framing is becoming the organizing intellectual framework for the Democratic left's foreign policy critique; it directly links military adventurism to domestic economic grievance and the political conditions that produced Trump.
insights.quote: In those last 30 years, we've had over 20 years of failed military interventions, $3 trillion, three million combat tours, over 7,000 of our own dead, tens of thousands of others dead. And what's not in those numbers is the unequal burden that was borne by the working class.

insights.claim: Duss approvingly cites JD Vance's 2024 RNC speech as an effective articulation of elite failure for working-class communities — de-industrialization, NAFTA, the Iraq War lie — and argues Democrats must produce an equivalent narrative with an actual governing vision, not just a critique.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: The left's willingness to credit Vance's political effectiveness creates strategic pressure on Democratic moderates who dismissed the Trump coalition's economic grievances — and raises the ceiling for how far left a 2028 Democratic nominee can go on foreign policy while remaining electable.
insights.quote: In small towns like mine in Ohio or next door in Pennsylvania or Michigan... jobs were sent overseas and our children were sent to war.

insights.claim: Trump's war with Iran — fought alongside Israel — has discredited his anti-war positioning and created an opening Duss believes is the most significant in decades for an alternative foreign policy vision, since the Democratic Party is not going back to Bidenism and will not replicate Trumpism.
insights.speaker: Ezra Klein / Matt Duss
insights.so_what: The Iran war is the key political event enabling this foreign policy realignment; it directly undermines Trump's brand as the anti-war candidate and may make the anti-war lane fully available to Democrats for the first time since 2008.

themes.theme: Military-Industrial Complex, Arms Lobby, and Congressional War Powers

insights.claim: Duss identifies the arms lobby as a structural driver — not just an Israel-specific factor — behind U.S. failure to enforce existing arms export laws, naming Egypt and Saudi Arabia alongside Israel as countries that benefit from routine non-enforcement of the Leahy Law and Arms Export Control Act.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: The arms-lobby critique, if it gains political traction, threatens long-term revenue for U.S. defense contractors dependent on Middle East and Gulf sales — Raytheon, Lockheed, Boeing are implicitly in the crosshairs of this policy agenda.
insights.quote: The arms lobby is an extremely powerful one. There is a strong incentive to just kind of push these sales through.

insights.claim: Congressman Crow argues that Congress has ceded its constitutional war powers — over trade, treaties, war, and appropriations — to the executive for decades, and that reclaiming those powers is a prerequisite for any serious foreign policy reform; without it, any single president can override the entire framework.
insights.speaker: Jason Crow (via clip)
insights.so_what: Congressional war powers reform is the structural mechanism through which the left plans to constrain executive foreign policy; it has broad appeal across the political spectrum (Sanders, AOC, and some libertarian-right Republicans) and is the most likely near-term legislative vehicle.
insights.quote: Our founders believed that Congress had a fundamental role in our foreign policy from trade to treaties to war powers and to appropriations. For decades, Congress has ceded and given up many of those powers.

insights.claim: Duss notes that war powers resolutions have been introduced on Venezuela, Iran, and Cuba under Trump and that most Republicans have refused to vote for them — choosing not to own the foreign policy decisions — which he reads as a much deeper institutional problem than any single president.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: The refusal of Republicans to vote for war powers resolutions — even on clearly unauthorized conflicts — means structural war powers reform requires a Democratic trifecta; it also means individual Republican senators face no near-term electoral accountability for these wars.

insights.claim: Ezra Klein makes the observation that the paradox of American governance is that presidents are severely constrained on domestic policy by the filibuster and polarized Congress but have nearly unlimited unilateral authority on foreign/military policy — and this asymmetry creates an incentive for ambitious presidents to seek foreign policy legacy when domestic achievements are blocked.
insights.speaker: Ezra Klein
insights.so_what: This domestic-foreign power asymmetry is a structural driver of foreign policy adventurism regardless of party; war powers reform is the only structural fix, and it would require legislative action that itself faces the same filibuster constraint.

themes.theme: American Global Primacy vs. Positive-Sum Multilateralism

insights.claim: Duss argues that American global military hegemony is not delivering safety or prosperity proportional to its costs, and that the benefits accrue to a tiny unaccountable elite while the costs — de-industrialization, resource diversion, inequality — fall on the working class; he explicitly questions whether the U.S.-Israel relationship produces net benefits for the United States.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: This cost-benefit reframe of the entire post-WWII alliance structure — treating alliances as potentially net-negative for U.S. workers — is the intellectual foundation of a left foreign policy that would look radically different from both Biden and Clinton internationalism.
insights.quote: When I look at the costs and benefits both strategically, ethically, morally, politically, diplomatically to the US-Israel relationship, I don't think it works out in the US's favor.

insights.claim: Duss distinguishes between 'American primacy' (leadership) and 'American hegemony' (control) and argues the foreign policy establishment conflates the two — using the language of global responsibility to justify what is in practice an effort to dominate and exclude rivals, especially China.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: This primacy-vs-hegemony distinction, if adopted by a future Democratic administration, would restructure how the U.S. frames relations with China — moving from strategic competition framing toward managed coexistence, with implications for tariffs, tech controls, and Taiwan policy.

insights.claim: AOC at Munich argued that we were not in a post-rules-based order but in a 'pre-rules-based order' — the system has never actually operated by its stated rules for non-Western countries — and that the current moment is an opportunity to build one that genuinely upholds democracy, human rights, and trade that centers working-class people rather than the wealthy.
insights.speaker: AOC (via clip)
insights.so_what: This reframe positions the left not as wanting to restore the old order but as wanting to build the first genuinely equitable one — a more ambitious and harder-to-attack framing than 'multilateralism'; it will shape how a left-led administration approaches WTO reform, UN reform, and global trade negotiations.
insights.quote: I think it's possible that we were in a pre-rules-based order and we have an opportunity to explore what a world would look like if we upheld democracy, human rights, trade that actually centers working-class people.

insights.claim: Biden's 2021 Russia summit strategy — 'park Russia, focus on China' — reveals that the China competition obsession was the actual organizing principle of Biden's entire foreign policy, including his approach to Ukraine and the Middle East, not simply a regional or trade concern.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: If China-competition was the meta-frame for Biden's foreign policy, any successor strategy that de-prioritizes competitive framing vs. China would require a wholesale restructuring of the national security apparatus — not just policy tweaks.

insights.claim: Duss argues the U.S. should not define its relationship with China as 'competition' because competitive framing inevitably leads to conflict; he is 'less convinced' that China's goal is to supplant the United States and believes China is largely acting within the U.S.-built international order rather than trying to overthrow it.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: A left administration's China policy would likely move from the current 'strategic competition' framework toward managed coexistence — with downstream implications for tech export controls, tariff regimes, Taiwan arms sales, and Indo-Pacific military posture.
insights.quote: I think the problem with defining the relationship as competition is one that eventually will lead to conflict.

insights.claim: Duss notes that Trump, despite making China competition the centerpiece of his first term and Biden following suit, showed surprising conciliation toward China in his second term — interpreting this as evidence that Xi demonstrated China has 'cards to play' and that the U.S. 'simply cannot assert its will on China.'
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: The bipartisan recognition that China cannot simply be bullied into compliance weakens the domestic political rationale for aggressive trade and tech restrictions; it opens space for a more conciliatory posture that could affect tariff policy and semiconductor export controls.
insights.quote: Xi has shown him that China has cards to play. The United States simply cannot assert its will on China.

insights.claim: Duss criticizes the Biden administration for taking the 'foreign policy for the middle class' insight — correctly identifying neoliberal trade failures — but then deploying it as just another weapon in strategic competition with China rather than as the basis for a genuinely more equitable global trade order that lifts workers in both countries.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: This critique implies that Biden's industrial policy (IRA, CHIPS Act) was framed primarily as China competition rather than worker welfare — and that a true left trade policy would look different, possibly including cooperative labor standard frameworks rather than tariffs.
insights.quote: Having come back to the idea that yes, it is right and appropriate for governments to play a major role in shaping and guiding the economy, the question is to what end.

insights.claim: As an alternative to competitive China policy, Duss proposes a global minimum corporate tax (which the Biden administration did pursue) and a global minimum wage proposal (floated by Bernie Sanders) as tools to build a positive-sum global trade order — though he acknowledges he does not know what a global minimum wage would look like in practice for poor countries like Kenya.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: The global minimum wage proposal is intellectually underdeveloped (Duss admits he doesn't know what it looks like), which means it is likely to be a rhetorical aspiration rather than near-term policy — but it signals the direction of left trade thinking and may evolve into concrete proposals by 2028.
insights.quote: Still working on what it exactly looks like. But what I'm saying is proposing, you know, putting the United States in the position of we are not just there to extract wealth.

themes.theme: Elite Impunity, Corruption, and the Legitimation Crisis

insights.claim: Duss argues that 'elite impunity' — the sense that the wealthy and well-connected operate under a different set of rules — is the core of the current political crisis, and that this manifests in foreign policy through the absence of consequences for officials who carry out failed or criminal policies.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: The elite impunity frame connects domestic economic grievance to foreign policy accountability — it is the intellectual architecture linking Gaza reckoning, war powers reform, and anti-corruption policy into a unified left political project.
insights.quote: The sense that the wealthy, the powerful, the well-connected, the influential don't pay a price, they operate according to a different set of rules than the rest of us.

insights.claim: Duss argues the United States was already the 'most corrupt modern democracy' even before Trump — citing John Ossoff's framing — and that closing down international money laundering through South Dakota trusts and similar vehicles is a concrete near-term anti-corruption policy the U.S. can pursue unilaterally with the UK.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: South Dakota trusts as a kleptocracy destination is a specific, actionable legislative target; combined with UK cooperation, this could be the anti-corruption flagship policy of a future left Democratic administration.
insights.quote: Who knew that trusts in South Dakota would be one of the main ways that kleptocrats abroad hid their money, but South Dakota apparently very popular.

insights.claim: Duss identifies campaign finance reform — possibly requiring a constitutional amendment — as the domestic structural root of foreign policy capture, arguing that the arms lobby's ability to push through illegal arms sales flows from the same money-in-politics dynamic that produced the broader legitimation crisis.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: If the left successfully frames campaign finance reform as a foreign policy issue (not just a domestic governance issue), it could build a broader coalition; but the constitutional amendment pathway makes it a very long-term project.

insights.claim: Senator Chris Murphy's book 'The Crisis of the Common Good' argues that the loss of community, the sense that systems are unaccountable, and the extraction of wealth at every step are the underlying causes of the political crisis — and that foreign policy reform cannot be sustained without rebuilding this domestic political consensus.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss (recommending Murphy's book)
entities: The Crisis of the Common Good
insights.so_what: Murphy is positioning himself as the intellectual architect of a new Democratic foreign policy vision ahead of a potential 2028 presidential run; his book is the policy document to track.

themes.theme: Trade-offs: Cheap Goods, Cheap Oil, and the Limits of Values-Based Foreign Policy

insights.claim: Klein challenges Duss directly: the Biden administration put large tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles to protect American manufacturing jobs, but voters were simultaneously furious about high car prices — illustrating that a foreign policy for the working class faces irresolvable tension between protecting jobs and keeping tradable goods cheap.
insights.speaker: Ezra Klein
insights.so_what: The EV tariff case study is the sharpest empirical challenge to the left's trade agenda — any 2028 candidate who runs on protecting American manufacturing must have a credible answer to the consumer price impact, especially on EVs, where Chinese competition would materially lower costs.
insights.quote: We could have much cheaper electric vehicles in this country if we would let the Chinese electric vehicles in. The Biden administration put huge tariffs on those... but then also people were very mad about the cost of cars in that same period.

insights.claim: Klein raises the Saudi Arabia case as a structural example of how values-based foreign policy collapses when it conflicts with cheap oil: the Biden administration initially held Saudi Arabia at arm's length, then abandoned that posture when oil prices rose, demonstrating that energy prices reliably override foreign policy values commitments.
insights.speaker: Ezra Klein
insights.so_what: Until the U.S. reduces oil price sensitivity — through domestic energy production or demand destruction via EVs — values-based distancing from Gulf autocracies will remain politically unsustainable; this creates a structural dependency that the left's foreign policy must grapple with.

insights.claim: Duss acknowledges there is no clean answer to the jobs-vs.-cheap-goods trade-off and that the real solution requires a comprehensive social safety net overhaul so that workers are insulated from trade competition rather than needing protectionist barriers — but Klein calls this a 'dodge' on the direct tariff question.
insights.speaker: Matt Duss
insights.so_what: Duss's inability to give a direct answer on tariff trade-offs reveals the policy gap at the center of the left's foreign economic agenda — the 2028 candidate who solves this problem credibly will have a significant advantage.
